title: Unit circle (with radians)
description: Get more practice with the unit circle definition of sine and cosine, this time with radians instead of degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the value of the $x$-coordinate of point $A$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the process for determining the sine or cosine of any angle $\theta$:

1. Starting from the point $(1,0)$, move counterclockwise along the unit circle until the angle that is formed between your position, the origin, and the positive $x$-axis is equal to $\theta$.
2. The $x$-coordinate of that point is equal to $\cos(\theta)$ and the $y$-coordinate of that point is equal to $\sin(\theta)$.

According to this the definition, which angle corresponds to point $A$?

The angle (in radians) that corresponds to point $A$ is $\dfrac{2\pi}{3}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The $x$-coordinate of that point is $\cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)$.
In conclusion, the value of the $x$-coordinate of point $A$ is $\cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)$.

options.alt: A unit circle on an x y coordinate plane where the center of the unit circle is at the origin and the circumference of the circle touches (one, zero), (zero, one), (negative one, zero), and (zero, negative one). A point is located at near eleven o'clock on the circle. A line segment extends from (zero, zero) to Point A. An angle from the arc starting at (zero, one) and opening counter clockwise to Point A has an angle measurement of pi over six radians.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the value of the $y$-coordinate of point $A$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the process for determining the sine or cosine of any angle $\theta$:

1. Starting from the point $(1,0)$, move counterclockwise along the unit circle until the angle that is formed between your position, the origin, and the positive $x$-axis is equal to $\theta$.
2. The $x$-coordinate of that point is equal to $\cos(\theta)$ and the $y$-coordinate of that point is equal to $\sin(\theta)$.

According to this the definition, which angle corresponds to point $A$?

The angle (in radians) that corresponds to point $A$ is $\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The $y$-coordinate of that point is $\sin\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{4}\right)$.
In conclusion, the value of the $y$-coordinate of point $A$ is $\sin\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{4}\right)$.

options.alt: A unit circle on an x y coordinate plane where the center of the unit circle is at the origin and the circumference of the circle touches (one, zero), (zero, one), (negative one, zero), and (zero, negative one). Point A is located at near four thirty o'clock on the circle. A line segment extends from (zero, zero) to Point A. An angle from the arc starting at (one, zero) and opening clockwise to Point A has an angle measurement of pi over four radians.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the value of the $y$-coordinate of point $A$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the process for determining the sine or cosine of any angle $\theta$:

1. Starting from the point $(1,0)$, move counterclockwise along the unit circle until the angle that is formed between your position, the origin, and the positive $x$-axis is equal to $\theta$.
2. The $x$-coordinate of that point is equal to $\cos(\theta)$ and the $y$-coordinate of that point is equal to $\sin(\theta)$.

According to this the definition, which angle corresponds to point $A$?

The angle (in radians) that corresponds to point $A$ is $\dfrac{11\pi}{6}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The $y$-coordinate of that point is $\sin\left(\dfrac{11\pi}{6}\right)$.
In conclusion, the value of the $y$-coordinate of point $A$ is $\sin\left(\dfrac{11\pi}{6}\right)$.

options.alt: A unit circle on an x y coordinate plane where the center of the unit circle is at the origin and the circumference of the circle touches (one, zero), (zero, one), (negative one, zero), and (zero, negative one). Point A is located at near four o'clock on the circle. A line segment extends from (zero, zero) to point A. An angle from the arc starting at (one, zero) and opening clockwise to point A has an angle measurement of pi over six radians.

assessment_items.question_content:
**For each point on the unit circle, select the angle that corresponds to it.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints: To determine the point on the unit circle that corresponds to angle $\theta$, we start from the point $(1,0)$ and move counterclockwise along the circle until the angle that is formed between our position, the origin, and the positive $x$-axis is equal to $\theta$.

Let's take point $A$. The angle it forms with the positive $x$-axis is between $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ and $\pi$, so an appropriate value is $\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Point $B$ forms with the positive $x$-axis an angle that is between $\pi$ and $\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$, so an appropriate value is $\dfrac{9\pi}{7}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Point $C$ forms with the positive $x$-axis an angle that is between $\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$ and $2\pi$, so an appropriate value is $\dfrac{11\pi}{6}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

In conclusion, this is the correct answer:

Point | Angle
:-: | :-:
$A$ | $\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$
$B$ | $\dfrac{9\pi}{7}$
$C$ | $\dfrac{11\pi}{6}$

options.imageAlt: A unit circle on an x y coordinate plane where the center of the unit circle is at the origin and the circumference of the circle touches (one, zero), (zero, one), (negative one, zero), and (zero, negative one). Point A is located at near ten thirty o'clock on the circle. Point B is located near seven thirty o'clock on the circle. Point C is located near four o'clock on the circle.

assessment_items.question_content:
**For each point on the unit circle, select the angle that corresponds to it.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints: To determine the point on the unit circle that corresponds to angle $\theta$, we start from the point $(1,0)$ and move counterclockwise along the circle until the angle that is formed between our position, the origin, and the positive $x$-axis is equal to $\theta$.

Let's take point $A$. The angle it forms with the positive $x$-axis must be acute, so $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ would be an appropriate value for it.

[[☃ image 1]]

Point $B$ forms with the positive $x$-axis an angle that is slightly less than $\pi$, so an appropriate value is $\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Point $C$ forms with the positive $x$-axis an angle that is between $\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$ and $2\pi$, so an appropriate value is $\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

In conclusion, this is the correct answer:

Point | Angle
:-: | :-:
$A$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$
$B$ | $\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$
$C$ | $\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$

Point A is located at near one thirty o'clock on the circle. Point B is located near ten o'clock on the circle. Point C is located near four thirty o'clock on the circle.

[[☃ label-image 1]]

options.imageAlt: A unit circle on an x y coordinate plane where the center of the unit circle is at the origin and the circumference of the circle touches (one, zero), (zero, one), (negative one, zero), and (zero, negative one). Point A is located at near two o'clock on the circle. Point B is located near eight o'clock on the circle. Point C is located near five o'clock on the circle.

assessment_items.question_content:
**For each point on the unit circle, select the angle that corresponds to it.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints: To determine the point on the unit circle that corresponds to angle $\theta$, we start from the point $(1,0)$ and move counterclockwise along the circle until the angle that is formed between our position, the origin, and the positive $x$-axis is equal to $\theta$.

Let's take point $A$. The angle it forms with the positive $x$-axis is between $0$ and $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$, and it is closer to $0$ than $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$. So an appropriate value is $\dfrac{\pi}{5}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Point $B$ also forms with the positive $x$-axis an angle that is between $0$ and $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$. This time, the point is closer to $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$. So an appropriate value is $\dfrac{3\pi}{7}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Point $C$ forms with the positive $x$-axis an angle that is between $\pi$ and $\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$, so an appropriate value is $\dfrac{4\pi}{3}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

In conclusion, this is the correct answer:

Point | Angle
:-: | :-:
$A$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{5}$
$B$ | $\dfrac{3\pi}{7}$
$C$ | $\dfrac{4\pi}{3}$

options.imageAlt: A unit circle on an x y coordinate plane where the center of the unit circle is at the origin and the circumference of the circle touches (one, zero), (zero, one), (negative one, zero), and (zero, negative one). Point A is located at near two o'clock on the circle. Point B is located near twelve thirty o'clock on the circle. Point C is located near seven o'clock on the circle.

assessment_items.question_content:
Points $A$, $B$, and $C$ are plotted on the unit circle.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the coordinates is equal to $\cos\left(\dfrac{3\pi}{4}\right)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the process for determining the sine or cosine of any angle $\theta$:

1. Starting from the point $(1,0)$, move counterclockwise along the unit circle until the angle that is formed between your position, the origin, and the positive $x$-axis is equal to $\theta$.
2. The $x$-coordinate of that point is equal to $\cos(\theta)$ and the $y$-coordinate of that point is equal to $\sin(\theta)$.

According to this the definition, which point corresponds to an angle of $\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$ (in radians)?

Point $B$ corresponds to an angle of $\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Therefore, its $x$-coordinate will give us $\cos\left(\dfrac{3\pi}{4}\right)$.
In conclusion, the coordinate that is equal to $\cos\left(\dfrac{3\pi}{4}\right)$ is the $x$-coordinate of point $B$.
